justification: All pass criteria met: Authentication per consumer type (JWT for mobile, API key for partners) designed correctly with detailed Auth Module. Internal traffic separation fully addressed with separate internal listener, mTLS, and network-level isolation. Business logic explicitly excluded from gateway multiple times (component description, pitfalls_to_avoid). SPOF risk identified as critical severity with horizontal scaling mitigation. Webhook delivery for partners addressed as a dedicated async service with retry, signing, and dead-letter queue. flexibility_points and rigidity_points both present and well-reasoned. Technology recommendation covers multiple options (Kong, AWS API Gateway, NGINX with Lua, custom Node/Go) with explicit acknowledgment that choice depends on team size/expertise — build vs. buy consideration addressed via alternatives_considered in trade_offs.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough internal traffic separation — not just a separate listener but network-level isolation enforced via infrastructure policies, with explicit statement that gateway does NOT proxy internal-to-internal traffic
Business logic exclusion is reinforced in multiple places: component boundaries, pitfalls_to_avoid, and patterns_to_follow
Webhook delivery design is production-grade: HMAC-SHA256 signing, exponential backoff, dead-letter queue, separate scaling, async via message broker
Strangler fig migration strategy shows practical deployment thinking beyond just architecture design
Rate limiting design handles the nuance of fail-open vs fail-closed with a clear recommendation and reasoning
Rigidity points are genuinely rigid and correctly identified — the normalized header contract as an intentional anti-corruption layer boundary is insightful
Risk around API key leakage addressed with hashing at rest, rotation support, and no-logging policy — goes beyond the rubric requirements

Technology recommendation could more explicitly frame the build vs. buy trade-off as a discrete decision rather than listing options — the trade_offs section covers migration strategy but not gateway technology selection as its own trade-off

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Authentication per consumer type designed correctly (JWT for mobile, API key+HMAC for partners, none for internal). Internal traffic separation fully addressed via private listener with network-level isolation. Business logic explicitly excluded from gateway (stated multiple times including in pitfalls_to_avoid). SPOF risk identified as critical with mitigation. Webhook delivery for partners thoroughly addressed with dedicated Webhook Dispatcher service, retry logic, HMAC signing, and dead-letter handling. flexibility_points and rigidity_points both present and well-reasoned. Technology recommendation includes build/buy consideration (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Envoy mentioned with caveat that choice depends on team expertise).

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough internal traffic separation — network-level isolation via NetworkPolicy, private listener on separate port, no auth required, with defense-in-depth application checks
Business logic exclusion is hammered home repeatedly: in component boundaries, in pitfalls_to_avoid, and in patterns_to_follow
Webhook delivery design is production-grade: async via message bus, HMAC-signed payloads, exponential backoff, dead-letter queue, partner dashboard for visibility
Strangler Fig migration strategy is practical and well-phased across 10 weeks
Trade-offs are concrete with alternatives_considered for each decision, not just generic pros/cons
Rigidity points are intentional and justified rather than accidental constraints
Gateway latency quantified (target < 5ms p99) rather than hand-waved

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: JWT for mobile and API key for partners designed with separate ingress pipelines; internal traffic fully separated via dedicated service mesh with network isolation; business logic explicitly excluded from gateway multiple times; SPOF risk identified as critical with mitigation; webhook delivery addressed with dedicated async dispatcher including retries and dead-letter queue; flexibility_points and rigidity_points present and well-defined; technology recommendation includes Kong/Envoy/cloud-native with build vs buy consideration via strangler fig phased approach.

observations.text: Three distinct ingress pipelines (mobile, partner, internal) with tailored auth and rate limiting per consumer type
Business logic exclusion from gateway stated explicitly in component boundaries and pitfalls_to_avoid
Internal service mesh completely isolated at network level with mTLS, no shared gateway with external traffic
SPOF risk marked critical with concrete mitigation (horizontal scaling, health checks, circuit breakers)
Webhook dispatcher fully decoupled with exponential backoff, dead-letter queue, and partner-facing status dashboard
Technology guidance covers multiple options (Kong, Envoy, cloud-native) with acknowledgment that choice depends on team context
Strangler fig migration pattern avoids big-bang cutover risk
Redis rate limiter fail-open vs fail-closed trade-off explicitly addressed
Security considerations like API key hashing and header injection prevention covered in pitfalls

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"The logistics platform needs a unified API gateway layer to replace inconsistent per-service auth and rate limiting. The design introduces a single logical gateway with three distinct ingress paths (mobile, partner, internal), centralizing cross-cutting concerns while maintaining strict traffic isolation. This eliminates duplicated auth logic across services, enforces consistent rate limiting, and creates a clear boundary between external and internal traffic.","architectur
